title: How rituals work
subtitle: The five-part shape Rebecca uses across her twelve weekly money ceremonies.
excerpt: A reference article for the ritual practices in the library. Covers the five-part Prepare / Release / Allow / Integrate / Anchor structure, what each step does, and how to adapt a ritual to a different week or topic.
sourceNotes: The five-part Prepare / Release / Allow / Integrate / Anchor structure is Rebecca J Page's own framework, used across the twelve weekly rituals in The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (2025).

practice.whenToUse: Read once before working through any of the ritual practices in the library.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rebecca's ritual shape: Prepare, Release, Allow, Integrate, Anchor. Used across the twelve weekly ceremonies in The Money Journal.

content.text: Rituals in the library follow a five-part shape Rebecca uses across the twelve weekly ceremonies in The Money Journal (2025). The same shape works for a money reset, a wealth-identity activation, a forgiveness rite, a closing of one month and an opening of the next. Each step does a specific piece of work; the shape is what lets the rite fit ten or fifteen minutes.

The set-up. Sit somewhere quiet. Light a candle if you have one. Take a few slow breaths. Picture the specific thing the ritual is for (the week behind you, the money in the accounts, the relationship that needs tending) resting in front of you. Concrete, not abstract.

Say the release statement aloud or in the mind, three times. The statement names what the ritual is letting go of (fear, survival energy, a specific belief, an inherited pattern). Follows the Release / Allow shape from Rebecca's Money Zone; see
Say the allow statement aloud or in the mind, three times. The statement names what the ritual is making room for. Same shape as the release: first-person, present tense, positive construction.

One slow breath. An image specific to the ritual: golden light, warm water, a held bowl, or a candle flame. Sit with it for a minute. This step gives the body and the mind time to register what's just happened.
The single take-away sentence, written in your own handwriting on paper if you have it, said aloud if you don't. The anchor sentence is what carries the ritual forward into the week.

Ten to fifteen minutes for a typical ritual. Slower is better than faster: the integration step in particular needs space. The five parts can run shorter, but cutting integration usually means the ritual doesn't land.

Most rituals in the library need one or two of: a candle, a glass of water, a pen and paper, a small object (a coin, a note, a ring). The specific items are listed in each ritual's body. A candle is the most common; a glass of water substitutes when no candle is to hand.

The five-part shape is portable. To adapt a ritual from one topic to another, keep Prepare, Integrate, and Anchor; rewrite the Release and Allow statements to name the new topic. The Money Journal's twelve weekly rituals work this way: each week's ritual uses the same shape with a different release-and-allow pair drawn from that week's theme.

The five-part structure is Rebecca J Page's own framework, used across The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (2025). The Release / Allow statements inside the ritual draw on her Money Zone method (2024). The wider lineage of weekly ritual practice (taking five or ten minutes to close one week and open the next) predates Rebecca's work and appears across many contemplative and folk traditions; this library uses Rebecca's specific shape because it's tight enough to be daily-life-sized.
